data.title: GOV. JARED POLIS (D), COLORADO, IS INTERVIEWED ON MSNBC
data.description:
TRANSCRIPT

April 03, 2025

NEWS PROGRAM

GOV. JARED POLIS (D), COLORADO

GOV. JARED POLIS (D), COLORADO, IS INTERVIEWED ON MSNBC

VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

20 East Thomas Road, Suite 2200

Phoenix, AZ 85012

asc.info@viqsolutions.com

Copyright 2025. Provided under license from VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

All materials herein are protected by United States copyright law

and/or license from VIQ Media Transcription, Inc., and may not be

reproduced, distributed, transmitted, displayed, published or

broadcast without the prior written permission of

VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

You may not alter or remove any trademark, copyright or other

notice from copies of the content.

GOV. JARED POLIS (D), COLORADO, IS INTERVIEWED ON MSNBC

APRIL 3, 2025

SPEAKERS:

GOV. JARED POLIS (D), COLORADO

ALICIA MENENDEZ, HOST: Joining me now, Colorado Governor Jared Polis.

Governor, thanks for being with us. Your reaction to these tariffs and to the market sell-off?

GOV. JARED POLIS (D), COLORADO: It's a sad day, really, one of the saddest days when we're inflicting — the president of the United States is inflicting this much harm and damage and pain to people, over $3 trillion of value wiped out in the stock market in one day, over $8 trillion over the last few weeks because of people knowing he might do this. And that's money from people's pensions and retirement and savings. And it's just a sad day for our country and, frankly, also a sad day for the world.

MENENDEZ: Help me understand, when you talk to business owners in your state about — about this news, what is it they are telling you about the impact it is going to have on Colorado?

POLIS: So, you know, I think people understand right away the — the tax increase for families. It's about $2,100 per family. That's the Tax Foundation, non-partisan, that's how much it costs the average family. But what I think people need to understand, and we're already seeing, is how this will destroy the manufacturing base of our country. What I hear from companies is we're not going to increase our capacity in the United States. We're going elsewhere because we get parts and materials from other countries. If those are subject to tariffs, we're going to produce them elsewhere, whether it's Canada, whether it's Mexico, whether it's Europe.

So investment and capital and manufacturing jobs are going to increasingly flee our country. Prices will go up for consumers. And it will cause a huge recession for us and, of course, to a lesser extent, a recession for the world. It's a disastrous economic policy. And I just was hoping, like many were, that the president was using this as a negotiating tactic. But apparently he believes in a flawed theory that now, unfortunately, we're going to have a real world example of how tariffs destroy your manufacturing base, destroy jobs, and raise costs for people.

MENENDEZ: Especially because that's the opposite of what they're saying it's going to do. I want you to take a listen to — to Lutnick, just today, talking about what all of this is going to mean for American jobs.

(BEGIN VIDEO CLIP)

HOWARD LUTNICK, COMMERCE SECRETARY: When Donald Trump yesterday said we have about $5 trillion of committed investment to America, $5 trillion, that means people are going to start building factories right now. And that means all that construction work begins now. That starts to employ Americans today. You're going to see plants being built, factories rebuilding. All ships are going to be running hot across America now. You're going to see employment leaping starting today.

(END VIDEO CLIP)

MENENDEZ: Where is it? When do I start seeing it? And how are they accounting for the fact that manufacturers, let's say — let's say they wanted to — to re-up manufacturing in this country, and they wanted those jobs to be in this country, and they were even willing to contend with what you laid out, which is parts are manufactured in different places and then often assembled here. We're going to manufacture them here. If you were dealing with a world leader who could wake up tomorrow and change his mind, what incentive is there for you to make a capital investment in manufacturing in the United States?

POLIS: That's exactly right. I mean, what Secretary Lutnick said sounds wonderful. Tragically, it's delusional. And I think there are many members of the administration that I hope are not subject to this delusion that somehow capital is coming to invest. It's the opposite. Capital is being destroyed. We saw $3 trillion destroyed today alone of value.

But it also deters future investment because nobody knows the rules of the game. And with — with manufacturing in the United States, if they're going to be selling to a world market, they're going to make it somewhere else, they're going to move manufacturing overseas. And that's what we're already seeing, people threatening and talking about pulling their investments out, going elsewhere. And — and that's tragically what's already occurring.

So, again, people who have a delusional viewpoint will, you know, watch again as — as unemployment numbers rise, investment flees, people's savings and pensions go down. And, you know, I guess then hopefully they'll come to realization too late. I hope eventually they back off these tariffs. But the uncertainty will have already set in destroying investment and destroying capital formation.

MENENDEZ: I think you get it really right when you talk about the tax that this is going to mean for American families and the fact that that is how people are going to feel at first, because I think there are people who say, well, the market's the market, and perhaps I don't have exposure to the market. And it's like, no, we all do. And you're going to see this in the cost of goods. You're going to see this in inflationary measures. And you're also going to see it because our allies are no longer so sure that they are our allies.

I want you to take a listen to what the Canadian prime minister had to say today about Canada's relationship to the United States.

(BEGIN VIDEO CLIP)

MARK CARNEY, CANADIAN PRIME MINISTER: Our old relationship of steadily deepening integration with the United States is over. The 80-year-period when the United States embraced the mantle of global economic leadership, when it forged alliances rooted in trust and mutual respect, and championed the free and open exchange of goods and services is over.

(END VIDEO CLIP)

MENENDEZ: How do you explain to the America first crowd that America first is becoming America alone?

POLIS: Well, and it's becoming America last, as the rest of the world works together to build prosperity — prosperity and wealth and jobs. And apparently America not only chooses not to be part of it, to the detriment of our own people, but actively seeks to destroy wealth. We're a big market. We're not the only market in the world. Goods and manufacturing will go on. European nations will trade with one another. They'll trade with Asia. Americans on the sidelines, families are going to see the immediate increases in cost, $2,100 a year. That's everything from socks to shirts to groceries, starting right away. And too many American families will feel the pain of losing a job and the uncertainty around their employment.

MENENDEZ: There is — as with all things in this administration, there is the doing and then there is the undoing, right? Like, no matter what happens now, there is damage in terms of mutual respect, mutual trust that I don't want to say is irreparable, but has clearly experienced some harm. Let's say he wakes up in a week and he says, yeah, these aren't working, I don't want to do it. To whom will the rebuilding fall? And what will that rebuilding require to take, as the Canadian prime minister said, back the mantle of global economic leadership?

POLIS: So, two things. First of all, again, the uncertainty will set in and stay, the shadow of these actions will last long beyond the tariffs themselves. Let's hope he wakes up tomorrow, next week, realizes these are destroying our economy, reverses them. It will reverse some of the damage, but not all. I don't think you'd see the stock market, for example, recover the full $3 trillion. You might see it recover half. You might see it recover three quarters. Because people know that they're going to hesitate to invest in a country where they're at the whim of what side of the bed the president wakes up on in the morning, tariffs, not tariffs.

What are we — what are we going to do? And is our capital investment and millions of dollars in creating jobs going to be justified and stable in a country where we don't even know where we can get our parts and materials in to make the products that we're selling across the United States and across the world?

So this is going to be devastating for a generation. I hope it leads Congress and the Senate, there was a bipartisan bill dropped today, to take back the mantle of Congress being able to prevent tariffs from unilaterally — unilaterally being entered by a president. It will eventually take that kind of action to help provide the reliability that we need to attract the investment we need to actually grow our economy rather than shrink our economy and destroy jobs, which this tariff tax hike will do and already is doing.

MENENDEZ: Colorado Governor Jared Polis, thank you so much for getting us started.

END

data.title: Duxton Water Appoints Chairman; Plans to Internalize Senior Management Team
data.description:
Duxton Water appointed Independent Non-Executive Director Brendan Rinaldi as chairman, effective June 1, succeeding Edouard Peter, according to a Friday filing with the Australian bourse.

The company also proposed to internalize its senior management team through a transitional services agreement with investment manager Duxton Capital Australia (DCA), following a 12-month due diligence, the filing said.

The internalization is subject to shareholder approval at the annual general meeting on May 30.

Under the agreement, DCA will continue providing operational support until internal capabilities are fully established.

If shareholder approval is not granted, the existing investment management agreement will remain in effect until its expiration in 2026.

The internalization is not expected to have a material impact on the company's operation, per the filing.

data.title: Aussie glove maker Ansell stands to lose most with Trump's 'Liberation Day' tariffs
data.description:
Shares in Ansell reversed course on Friday after having tumbled 25% from their February highs in the previous session, as the company unveiled plans to fully offset the tariff increases through pricing.

The Australia-listed company, known for its medical gloves and surgical gowns, gained 2.6% in early trade on Friday after having plunged as much as 16.4% a day earlier following the announcement of harsher-than-expected U.S. levies that will reshape global trade flows.

Analysts had warned that Ansell is among the most exposed companies to the new tariff regime, given its extensive manufacturing footprint across affected countries.

However, Ansell said in a statement on Friday that it is maintaining its fiscal 2025 forecast for adjusted earnings per share in the range of 118 U.S. cents to 128 U.S. cents.

A White House annexure details the planned tariff hikes: 24% on imports from Malaysia, 37% on Thailand, 44% on Sri Lanka and 27% on India — all countries where Ansell operates major production facilities.

It also manufactures in Vietnam, one of the worst-hit countries by the new duties, and maintains a production plant in China, alongside smaller facilities in Portugal, Lithuania and Brazil.

"We expect Ansell will reassess its outsourcing strategy given the varying tariff rates across Southeast Asia, ranging from 46% in Vietnam to 24% in Malaysia," Jefferies' analysts noted.

Despite the immediate market reaction, Ansell has signaled confidence in its ability to mitigate the tariff impact.

During its half-year results, the company outlined plans to "substantially offset" the cost pressures through a combination of price increases, diversifying its production base and relocating some manufacturing outside China.

While these measures offer a roadmap to adaptation, analysts remain cautious. Craig Wong-Pan of RBC Capital Markets estimates that, without mitigation, the tariffs could pose a hit of A$220 million to A$230 million ($139.46 million to $145.80 million) to Ansell's 2026 earnings.

Citi echoed concerns from its peers, describing Ansell as the medical equipment maker most negatively exposed to the tariff shifts.

With profits and margins recovering after a prolonged oversupply of products, the company now faces a fresh test — whether it can successfully navigate these geopolitical headwinds without derailing its rebound.

($1 = 1.5775 Australian dollars)

data.title: SmartStop Self Storage REIT Executives Increase Holdings
data.description:
Reporter NameRelationshipTypeAmountSEC Filing
Johnson WaynePresident and CIOPurchase$90,000Form 4

Wayne Johnson, President and CIO of SmartStop Self Storage REIT, has recently acquired 3,000 shares of common stock at a price of $30.0 per share, resulting in a total investment of $90,000. Following this transaction, Johnson's direct ownership in the company includes 3,000 shares of Common Stock and 5,863 shares of Class A Common Stock.

Harold Perry, a Director at SmartStop Self Storage REIT, also purchased 3,000 shares of Common Stock on the same day, at the same price of $30.0 per share, totaling a $90,000 investment. Post-purchase, Perry directly owns 3,000 shares of Common Stock and 9,657 shares of Class A Common Stock.

data.title: Florida's Winter Garden Offering $70 Million of Bonds For Water System Improvements
data.description:
By Stephen Nakrosis

The City of Winter Garden, Fla., plans to sell $70 million of revenue bonds with proceeds slated to finance and reimburse the cost of improvements to the municipal water and wastewater system.

The Series 2025 Water and Wastewater Revenue Bonds will be comprised of both serial and term bonds, according to documents posted Thursday on MuniOS. The securities are preliminarily scheduled to mature from 2027 through 2045, and the term bonds in 2050 and 2054.

Pricing is scheduled for April 9, with closing set for April 30. A portion of the proceeds will also be used to capitalize a portion of the interest to be paid on the debt and to pay for issuance costs.

The bonds are backed by a lien and pledge on revenue and connection fees of the system.

Winter Garden's population increased 49% between 2010 and 2024, to reach about 51,495 people last year. The water and wastewater system serves about 59,000 residents.

Moody's rated the bonds Aa2, and S&P Global Ratings assigned them a rating of AA-.

J.P. Morgan is listed as the offering's lead manager.

-Write to Stephen Nakrosis at stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com

data.title: Rio Tinto's Spending With Australian Suppliers Up 10% in 2024; Shares Down 3%
data.description:
Rio Tinto Group raised its spending with Australian suppliers by 9.9% year on year to AU$17.7 billion in 2024, according to a Friday statement from the mining giant.

This involved more than 6,000 suppliers across the country. Over AU$926 million was spent with 182 indigenous businesses, an increase of 27% since 2023, Rio Tinto said.

Spending with local Australian suppliers rose nearly 15% to AU$1.3 billion.

The mining giant's shares were down nearly 3% in recent Friday trade.

data.title: Nikkei May Fall as U.S. Tariffs Raise Concerns About Global GrowthMarket Talk
data.description: Japanese stocks may fall as new U.S. tariffs raise concerns about the pace of global growth. Nikkei futures are down 2.0% at 34020 on the SGX. USD/JPY is at 146.10, down from 147.25 as of Thursday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on any steps governments around the world are taking in response to the U.S. tariffs. The Nikkei Stock Average fell 2.8% to 34735.93 on Thursday. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

JAPAN FEB AVERAGE REAL HOUSEHOLD INCOME -2.3% Y/Y (JAN -1.1%), 2ND STRAIGHT DROP AS INFLATION BITES; +1.9% IN NOMINAL TERMS (JAN +3.5%)
Morgan Stanley scraps 2025 Fed rate cut forecast, cites Trump tariffs and inflation risks
data.description:
Morgan Stanley has withdrawn its forecast for a U.S. Federal Reserve rate cut in June, citing rising inflation risks stemming from Trump’s newly announced tariffs. The bank now expects the Fed to hold rates steady through to March 2026.

Trump announced sweeping tariffs on US imports on Wednesday, higher duties targeting dozens of countries. Morgan Stanley warned these measures could fuel "tariff-induced inflation," making it unlikely the Fed will proceed with any rate cuts this year.

Previously, the Wall Street firm had anticipated a 25 basis point cut in June. However, with inflationary pressures now expected to remain elevated, the firm believes the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) will delay any policy easing until inflation stabilises.

*

This conflicts with other analysts expecting Fed cuts on the back of weakening US economic growth (even recession) with the tariff hit.

data.title: Gold Steady, Supported by Potential Safe-Haven DemandMarket Talk
data.description: Gold is steady in the early Asian session, supported by potential safe-haven demand. "The asset could remain on a bullish trend as investors could continue to seek safety in the wake of President Trump's tariff announcement," Exness' Inki Cho says in an email. The tariffs have damped investor sentiment and spurred demand for safe-haven assets such as gold, the financial markets strategist adds. Spot gold is little changed at $3,114.02/oz. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Shareholders of AppLovin Corporation Should Contact Levi & Korsinsky Before May 5, 2025 to Discuss Your Rights – APP
data.description:
NEW YORK, NY / ACCESS Newswire / April 3, 2025 / If you suffered a loss on your AppLovin Corporation investment and want to learn about a potential recovery under the federal securities laws, follow the link below for more information:

https://zlk.com/pslra-1/applovin-corporation-lawsuit-submission-form?prid=140876&wire=1

or contact Joseph E. Levi, Esq. via email at jlevi@levikorsinsky.com or call (212) 363-7500 to speak to our team of experienced shareholder advocates.

THE LAWSUIT: A class action securities lawsuit was filed against AppLovin Corporation that seeks to recover losses of shareholders who were adversely affected by alleged securities fraud between May 10, 2023 and February 25, 2025.

CASE DETAILS: According to the complaint, defendants provided investors with material information concerning AppLovin's financial growth and stability. Defendants' statements included, among other things, confidence in AppLovin's launch of its AXON 2.0 digital ad platform and using "cutting-edge AI technologies" to more efficiently match advertisements to mobile games, in addition to expanding into web-based marketing and e-commerce. Moreover, defendants publicly reported impressive financial results, outlooks, and guidance to investors, all while using dishonest advertising practices.

The truth emerged on February 26, 2025, when analyst research reports emerged stating that AppLovin was reverse engineering and exploiting advertising data from Meta Platforms. The reports further alleged AppLovin was utilizing manipulative practices to artificially inflate their own ad click-through and app download rates, such as by having ads click on themselves or utilizing design gimmicks to trigger forced shadow downloads, erroneously inflating installation numbers and, in turn, its profit figures.

Following this news, the price of AppLovin's stock declined from $377.06 per share on February 25, 2025 to $331.00 per share on February 26, 2025.

WHAT'S NEXT? If you suffered a loss in AppLovin stock during the relevant time frame - even if you still hold your shares - go to https://zlk.com/pslra-1/applovin-corporation-lawsuit-submission-form?prid=140876&wire=1 to learn about your rights to seek a recovery. There is no cost or obligation to participate.

WHY LEVI & KORSINSKY: Over the past 20 years, Levi & Korsinsky LLP has established itself as a nationally-recognized securities litigation firm that has secured hundreds of millions of dollars for aggrieved shareholders and built a track record of winning high-stakes cases. The firm has extensive expertise representing investors in complex securities litigation and a team of over 70 employees to serve our clients. For seven years in a row, Levi & Korsinsky has ranked in ISS Securities Class Action Services' Top 50 Report as one of the top securities litigation firms in the United States. Attorney Advertising. Prior results do not guarantee similar outcomes.

data.title: JAPAN FEB HOUSEHOLD DATA: MAIN BREAD EARNERS’ AVG. REAL INCOME POSTS 1ST Y/Y FALL IN 5 MOS (-0.1%), THEIR SPOUSES’ INCOME IN 2ND STRAIGHT DROP (-7.6%)
Sankyo Tateyama to Cut 100 Jobs in Germany, Sell Part of Bonn Plant
data.description:
Sankyo Tateyama will restructure its European operations by ceasing rail component machining and welding at its Bonn plant in Germany, leading to a workforce reduction of about 100 employees, according to a Thursday filing on the Tokyo Stock Exchange.

The Japanese aluminum and building materials company expects to book a one-time loss of 1.2 billion yen in severance and related costs in the fiscal year ending May 2026 but anticipates a 1.5 billion yen earnings improvement from fiscal 2027 onward.

As part of the restructuring, the company will sell part of the Bonn plant's land and buildings to German public transport firm Univers Reisen GmbH, expecting a 2 billion yen gain on the sale.

The deal is set to close by May 2025.

data.title: JAPAN FEB REAL CORE HOUSEHOLD SPENDING (EX-HOUSING, VEHICLES, GIFT MONEY) -0.8% Y/Y VS. -0.8% IN JAN WHEN OVERALL SPENDING ROSE 0.8%
Avita Medical's Cohealyx Wound Treatment Makes Commercial Launch in US; Shares Down 4%
data.description:
Avita Medical launched wound care treatment Cohealyx in the US, according to a Friday filing with the Australian bourse.

Cohealyx is a collagen-based dermal matrix branded by Avita Medical and co-developed with Regenity Biosciences, the filing stated. It is designed to provide wound bed through cellular migration and revascularization for faster closure.

Avita Medical is enrolling participants in the Cohealyx I post-market trial.

The company's shares were down almost 4% in recent Friday trade.

data.title: Japan Personal Spending Drops Less than Expected
data.description:
Household spending in Japan fell by 0.5% yoy in February 2024, a reversal from a 0.8% increase in the prior month.

This was the first decline since November and came in better than market estimates of a 1.7% drop.

Monthly, personal spending increased by 3.5%, reversing January's 4.5% decline and beating forecasts of a 0.5% gain.

It also represented the strongest growth since March 2022.

data.title: Carlton Investments Buys Back Over 1,000 Shares
data.description:
Carlton Investments bought back 1,250 shares in an on-market buyback Thursday for AU$38,878 in total, according to a Friday Australian bourse filing.

The company plans to buy back shares worth AU$1 million, per the filing.

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1940 ET - Australia's Ansell could face intensifying competition while it implements changes to its sourcing and pricing agreements, Jefferies analysts say. They tell clients in a note that it will take time for the personal-protective equipment manufacturer to implement its response to U.S. tariffs. Writing prior to Ansell's announcement that it will hike its U.S. prices to offset tariff impacts, the Jefferies analysts observe that the company has greatest pricing power in categories where it has leading market share. They also reckon that Ansell has flexibility with outsourced products to pull supply from lower-tariffed countries. Jefferies has a last-published hold rating and A$42.00 target price on the stock, which is up 3.75% at A$30.44. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1925 ET [Dow Jones]--Guess' fiscal 2026 outlook, which calls for sales to increase up to about 6% from last year, assumes a stable consumer environment with no further material deterioration or significant upturn in demand, CFO Dennis Secor says on a call with analysts. "We are expecting that in general, consumers will remain cautious in their spending, given concerns over returning inflationary pressures and tariff issues," he says. "On top of that, both the war in Ukraine and the Red Sea crisis are not resolved, certainly impacting the European consumer and global supply chain." Despite these headwinds, the company experienced a modest improvement in retail trends during the first few weeks of its current quarter, which it attributed to recent marketing initiatives. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1907 ET - Guess is focusing its cost-savings efforts on China and North America, CEO Carlos Alberini says on a call with analysts. The company expects its Greater China business to lose $20 million this year. "In spite of how challenging this market has been for us over the years, we continue to believe that there is an opportunity for the Guess brand in Greater China as our brand awareness is high and the market is very large and compelling," Alberini says. As a result, the company plans to turn the business over to a third party, a position for which it has already met with several potential candidates. Guess also intends to shutter roughly 20 underperforming stores in North America this year. "Taking together, these two initiatives are expected to unlock over $30 million in operating profit, starting with the next fiscal year," Alberini says. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1859 ET - Guess CEO Carlos Alberini says on a call with analysts that the retailer has a plan to improve its in-store shopping experience. This strategy, however, starts online: "We know that 80% of the customers visiting stores perform extensive research online prior to their visits," he says. "So having a meaningful presence in social media is critical to influence customer choice and behavior." In brick and mortar, Guess is currently developing a new store concept that will include more exclusive products, a better store-clustering model that accounts for price, weather and occasion, and enhanced visual merchandising. "Most of these initiatives should also benefit our e-commerce business," he says. Some initiatives will likely roll out in the first half of the year, though most are expected to hit in the latter half. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1852 ET [Dow Jones]--Guess logged higher sales in its recent quarter, though profit fell, largely due to increased marketing expenses. CEO Carlos Alberini says on a call with analysts that marketing spend more than doubled year over year. "These investments are designed to build stronger brand awareness and enhance customer engagement," he says. Domestically, the retailer aimed to support Rag & Bone, which it recently acquired, as well as its namesake brand. Abroad, the company's marketing spend was focused on its Guess Jeans brand. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1833 ET - Australia's S&P/ASX 200 looks set for another substantial decline after the Trump administration's tariff announcement pushed U.S. equities to their worst day in more than five years. ASX futures are down by 1.2% ahead of Friday's session, pointing to a further drop for the benchmark index on top of Thursday's 0.9% slide. The ASX 200 is down by 1.5% so far this week, with shares of U.S.-exposed companies leading the decline. Ahead of the open, automotive-accessory provider Amotiv said it was considering options including re-sourcing supply or raising prices. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1804 ET - New Zealand's NZX-50 lost 1.2% inside the first hour of Friday's session, suggesting that the tariff-driven sell-off of regional equities is set to extend into a second day. Financial, consumer, real-estate and aged-care stocks were all in the red as the benchmark index, which was coming off a narrow gain, opened lower and kept slipping. Among the largest losers was The a2 Milk Company, which has been trying to expand its infant-formula sales into the U.S. The stock was down 4.8%. Recreational-vehicle rental provider Tourism Holdings slumped 5.2% after warning of weaker consumer sentiment toward vacationing in the U.S., where it operates. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1801 ET - A New York City fashion wholesaler says that President Trump's tariffs threaten calamity for his business. "It looks like this will be the first time in 45 years that we will lose money," Eddie Azar, president of Fantasia Accessories, tells WSJ via email. "I spent years getting out of China and moved production to Cambodia and Vietnam and now those countries are not feasible to do business in." Tariffs will balloon Fantasia's costs on contract work for which the firm has already locked in its selling prices; margins could hit minus 25% or minus 30% on unfilled orders. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1638 ET - Toy companies are highly exposed to President Trump's new sweeping tariffs, which are set to further raise levies on China while adding starkly higher ones elsewhere. Hasbro, which makes Nerf blasters and Play-Doh, appears to be the best positioned, thanks to sourcing some of its high-margin Wizards of the Coast products from the U.S., Jeffries analysts say in a research note. Rival Mattel, which makes Barbie dolls and Hot Wheels cars, has the most exposure to Southeast Asia, with principal manufacturing sites in Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand and Vietnam. Canadian toy maker Spin Master "appears fairly middle ground with the exposure to the EU," analysts write. Hasbro shares sank 12%, Mattel shares tumbled 17% and Spin Master shares fell 9%. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1606 ET - U.S. stocks dive on worries the President Trump's tariff plan will hurt global economic growth as trading partners get set to fight back. Information technology, consumer discretionary and energy sectors all drop around 5%, while consumer staples is the only sector to end with a sizable gain. Apple loses 9.3%, Amazon drops 9% and Nvidia falls 7.8%, with all members of the Magnificent 7 lower. The dollar and Treasury yields drop to the lowest levels of the year with oil and gold declining as well. DJIA drops 1679 points, or 4%, to 40545, the S&P 500 sinks 4.8% to 5396 and the Nasdaq dives 6% to 16550. (patrick.sullivan@wsj.com)

1509 ET - A spate of state-level bans or labeling requirements tied to food additives isn't a "material concern" for Conagra, CEO Sean Connolly says on a call with analysts. More than 90% of the Slim Jim and frozen-dinner maker's product portfolio doesn't have synthetic dyes or food colorings, the CEO says. The company will have to monitor the situation, because state-by-state legislation can create challenges for manufacturers, he says. "But with respect to food dyes and colors in general, we don't have a lot of it in our portfolio," Connolly says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1439 ET - While U.S. tariffs exclude most energy imports, they are likely to give U.S. natural gas a competitive advantage over the future development of wind and solar power,Gelber & Associates says in a note. The U.S. imported 54 gigawatts of solar panels in 2024, "with the majority coming from Southeast Asian countries that are set to face the highest tariffs under Trump's plan." The advantage could be offset, however, by higher cost of steel and other manufactured parts required to build out large natural gas infrastructure, the firm adds. Natural gas is up 1.6% at $4.121/mmBtu while oil and refined products slide. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Ansell Could Face Competition While It Amends PricesMarket Talk
data.description: Australia's Ansell could face intensifying competition while it implements changes to its sourcing and pricing agreements, Jefferies analysts say. They tell clients in a note that it will take time for the personal-protective equipment manufacturer to implement its response to U.S. tariffs. Writing prior to Ansell's announcement that it will hike its U.S. prices to offset tariff impacts, the Jefferies analysts observe that the company has greatest pricing power in categories where it has leading market share. They also reckon that Ansell has flexibility with outsourced products to pull supply from lower-tariffed countries. Jefferies has a last-published hold rating and A$42.00 target price on the stock, which is up 3.75% at A$30.44. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: Zscaler Director Sells 10,000 Shares
data.description:

RelationshipDirector

Andrew William Fraser Brown, a Director at Zscaler, sold 10,000 shares of Common Stock on April 2, 2025, at a price of $206.0 per share, totaling $2,060,000. Following this transaction, Brown directly owns 27,216 shares of Zscaler.

SEC Filing: Zscaler, Inc. [ ZS ] - Form 4 - Apr. 03, 2025

data.title: Kratos Defense & Security Solutions Executives Sell Shares
data.description:
Reporter NameCarter David M
RelationshipPresident, DRSS Division
TypeSell
Amount$117,114
SEC FilingForm 4

David M. Carter, President of the DRSS Division at Kratos Defense & Security Solutions, sold 4,000 shares of common stock on April 1, 2025, at a weighted average price of $29.2785, totaling $117,114. Following the transaction, Carter directly owns 109,425 shares of the company. The sale was conducted under a 10b5-1 trading plan adopted on May 20, 2024. The ownership includes shares purchased through the Employee Stock Purchase Plan and held through the 401(k) Plan.

SEC Filing: KRATOS DEFENSE & SECURITY SOLUTIONS, INC. [ KTOS ] - Form 4 - Apr. 03, 2025

data.title: Lost Money on Aldeyra Therapeutics, Inc.(ADLX)? Contact Levi & Korsinsky Regarding an Ongoing Investigation
data.description:
NEW YORK, NY / ACCESS Newswire / April 3, 2025 / Levi & Korsinsky notifies investors that it has commenced an investigation of Aldeyra Therapeutics, Inc. ("Aldeyra Therapeutics, Inc.") (NASDAQ:ADLX) concerning possible violations of federal securities laws.

On April 3, 2025, The company disclosed that the FDA issued a second Complete Response Letter (CRL) for reproxalap, Aldeyra's lead dry eye disease drug candidate. The FDA's CRL cited a failure to demonstrate efficacy for reproxalap and flagged concerns with the design of Aldeyra's most recent trial, including differences in baseline symptom scores that may have impacted the reliability of their trial results.

Following this news, shares of Aldeyra Therapeutics, Inc. fell over 74% in intraday trading. To obtain additional information, go to:

https://zlk.com/pslra-1/aldeyra-therapeutics-inc-lawsuit-submission-form?prid=140875&wire=1

or contact Joseph E. Levi, Esq.

WHY LEVI & KORSINSKY: Over the past 20 years, Levi & Korsinsky LLP has established itself as a nationally-recognized securities litigation firm that has secured hundreds of millions of dollars for aggrieved shareholders and built a track record of winning high-stakes cases. The firm has extensive expertise representing investors in complex securities litigation and a team of over 70 employees to serve our clients. For seven years in a row, Levi & Korsinsky has ranked in ISS Securities Class Action Services' Top 50 Report as one of the top securities litigation firms in the United States. Attorney Advertising. Prior results do not guarantee similar outcomes.

CONTACT:

Levi & Korsinsky, LLP

Joseph E. Levi, Esq.

Ed Korsinsky, Esq.

SOURCE: Levi & Korsinsky, LLP

data.title: Australian tech stocks fall to lowest levels in 8 months
data.description:
** Australian tech stocks dive as much as 4.5% to their lowest levels since early August 2024

** Shares track steep losses overnight on Wall Street with all three indexes logging their biggest one-day drop since 2020

** Sector majors Xero drops 3% while WiseTech Global tumbles 2.7%

** Sub-index down nearly 22% YTD vs a 5.2% drop in ASX 200 benchmark

data.title: Mexico says it will continue 'cool-headed' approach to Trump's tariffs
data.description:
Mexico's "cool-headed" approach to U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff offensive has paid off in preferential treatment in his sweeping tariff announcement this week and a close working relationship with his trade team, Mexico's deputy economy secretary told Reuters on Thursday.

Luis Rosendo Gutierrez, undersecretary for international trade, said in an interview that Mexican officials will meet next week with Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick and U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer to review U.S. tariffs on auto imports and steel and aluminum and the state of those industries.

data.title: JAPAN FEB REAL HOUSEHOLD SPENDING -0.5% Y/Y (JAN +0.8%), 1ST DROP IN 3 MONTHS; MEDIAN FORECAST -3.4% (RANGE: -4.6% TO -0.5%)
Maritime Announces Issuance of Shares in Satisfaction of Interest Payment Obligations
data.description:
Toronto, Ontario--(Newsfile Corp. - April 3, 2025) - Maritime Resources ("Maritime" or the "Company") is pleased to announce that, in connection with its previously announced contemplated issuance of common shares (the "Interest Shares") to holders of US$5,000,000 non-convertible senior secured notes issued on August 14, 2023 (the "Notes"), the Company has issued 2,573,090 Interest Shares in full satisfaction of the interest payable thereunder as of March 31, 2025 in the aggregate amount of US$129,500 (the "Interest Payment").

In accordance with the terms of the Notes, the Company issued the Interest Shares in satisfaction of the Interest Payment at a deemed price of C$0.072 per Interest Share. The issuance of the Interest Shares remains subject to the final approval of the TSX Venture Exchange.

In connection with the Interest Payment, the Company issued an aggregate of 514,618 Interest Shares to Dundee Corporation ("Dundee") and, accordingly, the issuance of such Interest Shares to Dundee constituted a "related party transaction" within the meaning of Multilateral Instrument 61-101 - Protection of Minority Shareholder Approval ("MI 61-101"). The Company has relied on exemptions from the formal valuation and minority shareholder approval requirements under MI 61-101 as neither the fair market value of the Interest Shares issued to Dundee, nor the consideration received for such Interest Shares, exceeded 25% of the Company's market capitalization. The Company did not file a material change report more than 21 days prior to the election to issue the Interest Shares as the period required to determine the number and per share price of the Interest Shares only ended on March 27, 2025.

Additional details regarding the Notes can be found in the Company's news releases dated July 27, 2023, August 21, 2023, and March 27, 2025, each of which is available under the Company's profile on SEDAR+ at www.sedarplus.ca.

About Maritime Resources Corp.

Maritime (OTC Pink: MRTMF) is a gold exploration and development company focused on advancing the Hammerdown Gold Project in the Baie Verte District of Newfoundland and Labrador, a top tier global mining jurisdiction. Maritime holds a 100% interest directly and subject to option agreements entitling it to earn 100% ownership in the Green Bay Property, which includes the former Hammerdown gold mine and the Orion gold project. Maritime controls over 439 km2 of exploration land including the Green Bay, Whisker Valley, Gull Ridge and Point Rousse projects. Mineral processing assets owned by Maritime in the Baie Verte mining district include the Pine Cove mill and the Nugget Pond gold circuit.

data.title: JAPAN GOVT: FEB REAL HOUSEHOLD SPENDING WOULD HAVE RISEN 1.8% Y/Y IF BOOSTING IMPACT OF LUNAR NEW YEAR IN FEB 2024 WERE EXCLUDED
Morgan Stanley raising about $680 million for Japan real estate fund, sources say
data.description:
Morgan Stanley is raising about 100 billion yen ($684 million) for a Japan-focused real estate fund, two people familiar with the matter said, highlighting investor interest in property as the world's fourth-largest economy emerges from years of deflation and standstill prices.

The Morgan Stanley-managed fund is due to close in June and expected to raise at least 100 billion yen based on current investor commitments, according to the people, both of whom declined to be identified because the information hasn't been made public. The final size of the fund could change by the closing, the people said.

Investment will centre on offices and multi-family residential buildings in major cities, as well as logistics and hotels, one of the people said.

Morgan Stanley declined to comment. Details of the fund are reported here for the first time.
